raw_case:
Hemorrhagic Bullous Lichen Sclerosus of the Breast: A Rare Complication of Radiotherapy
Cutaneous side effects from radiotherapy are commonly reported in breast cancer patients. Radiation-induced hemorrhagic bullous lichen sclerosus (RHBLS) of the breast is a rare, but important, complication of radiotherapy. RHBLS typically presents as painful, hemorrhagic bullae with surrounding sclerotic tissue. A 71-year-old female with a history of whole breast radiotherapy for invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast and ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) of the left breast presented to the clinic with pruritic, firm, and erythematous plaques involving the inframammary folds that progressed into large, painful, hemorrhagic bullae surrounded by porcelain-white skin over several weeks. Biopsy was consistent with RHBLS. She was initially treated with systemic steroids and Clobetasol 0.05% ointment twice daily with partial improvement. To the best of our knowledge, only three definitive cases have been previously reported in the literature making this the fourth case.

Introduction

Lichen sclerosus (LS) is a chronic inflammatory skin disease characterized by atrophic, white-to-porcelain papules and plaques that can cause significant pruritus and discomfort. Although genetic and autoimmune components have been suspected in the pathophysiology of LS, trauma-induced LS has also been reported [1,2]. The first report of LS affecting the breasts following radiotherapy was published in 1985 [3]. The first case of radiation-induced hemorrhagic bullous LS (RHBLS), a rare subtype of radiation-induced LS characterized by flaccid bullae and purpuric hemorrhage, involving the breasts was reported in 1994 [4]. Herein and to the best of our knowledge, we report the fourth known case of RHBLS of the breasts in a patient with a history of radiotherapy-treated breast cancer and emphasize the importance of early recognition, evaluation for genital involvement, and treatment to avoid further complications. As only three other cases have been documented, only limited conclusions can be drawn, emphasizing the importance of continued investigation into RHBLS.This article was previously presented as a meeting abstract at the American Academy of Dermatology's 2022 Gross and Microscopic Symposium.

Case presentation

A 71-year-old female with a history of invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast (2011) and ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) with microinvasive carcinoma of the left breast (2019), cholelithiasis, hyperlipidemia, and carpal tunnel syndrome presented in March 2021 with pruritic, firm, and erythematous plaques in both inframammary folds. Treatment for suspected intertrigo with a combination of topical steroids, nystatin, and zinc oxide was unsuccessful. Over the ensuing weeks, the breasts, as well as the inframammary folds, developed progressively large, painful, hemorrhagic bullae surrounded by porcelain-white skin as noted on physical exam (Figure 1). She was noted to have sclerotic changes in regions outside the field of radiation, including the axillae and inguinal folds. For the invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast, the patient underwent a partial mastectomy, followed by whole breast radiotherapy (4,800 grays (Gy) with 1,200 centiGrays (cGy) boost) in 2011 (Figure 2) and was treated with anastrozole from 2011 to 2016. In 2019 after an abnormal mammogram, she was found to have DCIS with microinvasive carcinoma of the left breast that was treated with lumpectomy, followed by whole breast radiotherapy (4,526 Gy with 1,000 cGy boost) of the left breast, as well as some lower radiation to the right breast (Figure 3), and letrozole therapy. Given the history of radiotherapy and physical exam findings, biopsies were performed to evaluate for RHBLS, bullous morphea, angiosarcoma, and chronic radiation dermatitis. The biopsies of a hemorrhagic bulla and sclerotic skin from the left breast resulted in a pauci-immune subepidermal bulla and lichen sclerosus, respectively (Figure 4). Taken collectively with the clinical exam, the patient was diagnosed with RHBLS. She was initially treated with systemic steroids and clobetasol 0.05% ointment bis in die (BID) with partial improvement at follow-up two months later.

Discussion

Cutaneous side effects from radiotherapy have been reported in up to 90% of breast cancer patients [5]. RHBLS of the breast is a rare, yet significant, complication of radiotherapy. To our knowledge, only three definitive cases have been previously reported in the literature [2,4,5] (Table 1). Trattner et al. reported a case of RHBLS of the breast of a 57-year-old female diagnosed with infiltrating ductal carcinoma three years after radiotherapy at a site of previously diagnosed morphea [4]. Peterson et al. reported a case of a 57-year-old female with unilateral RHBLS that presented three months after radiotherapy for invasive ductal carcinoma and eventually progressed to involve both breasts and vulva despite no radiotherapy to the left breast or vulva [5]. Bonfill-Ortí et al. reported RHBLS involving the right breast of a 71-year-old female two years after radiotherapy and letrozole for infiltrating the lobular carcinoma of the right breast [2]. Walsh et al. reported a case of hemorrhagic bullous LS in a 65-year-old woman with a history of breast cancer; however, there was no mention of any history of radiotherapy [6]. As radiotherapy is commonly used as primary and adjuvant treatment in cancer, it is likely that their case also represents RHBLS.RHBLS typically presents as painful, hemorrhagic bullae with surrounding sclerotic tissue. Involvement of skin outside the field of radiation, as noted in our patient, can be a feature seen in both radiation-induced LS and morphea but is atypical for other radiation-induced dermatoses [5,6]. If found to have RHBLS, a full body skin exam would be recommended to evaluate for other sites to rule out genital involvement [5]. We favored radiation as the most likely etiology as the lesions started on the breast prior to expanding to areas outside the radiation field as reported in the literature [5,6]; however, friction from wearing a bra could also be a contributory factor [5] but would not explain the involvement outside the treatment area. Clinically, RHBLS can overlap with bullous morphea including hemorrhagic bullae, necessitating a biopsy; however, porcelain-white skin is characteristic of LS. Histologically, morphea is characterized by excessive collagen deposition, which was not seen in our patient [4]. On histology, RHBLS is characterized by epidermal atrophy with orthokeratosis, a band of edematous, homogenized collagen in the papillary dermis with a dense degree of lymphocytic infiltrates, and subepidermal bullae [1,2].Regarding pathophysiology, koebernization from trauma, including radiation, has been reported [1,3,5]. Only Bonfill-Ortí et al. and our case report any treatment for breast cancer other than surgical intervention and radiation, suggesting that hormonal therapy is less likely to be the underlying trigger for RHBLS [2]. Radiation is thought to induce a TH2 immune response leading to increased fibroblast activity and sclerosis by upregulating IL-4 and TGF-beta, which can cause LS [5]. It is hypothesized the bullae that develop are because of alteration of the lymphatic vessels secondary to radiation [1]. The radiation regimen, including dose, fractionation, and boost, does not appear to correlate with the risk of developing RHBLS [5]. Treatment for RHBLS includes oral or high-potency topical corticosteroids as initial therapy [1,5]. Other therapies include topical calcineurin inhibitors, intralesional steroid injections, methotrexate, and phototherapy [1,2]. Symptoms and bullae have shown improvement in as little as three weeks [1].Other diagnoses to consider include chronic radiation dermatitis and angiosarcoma. Chronic radiation dermatitis can also present with atrophic areas after radiation, but histopathology shows atypical fibroblasts scattered singly in a background of thickened collagen bundles with superficially located dilated vessels containing swollen endothelial cells [7-9]. Angiosarcomas can occur postradiation and may present as violaceous plaques or nodules, but histopathology highlights an underlying irregular vascular process [9,10].

Conclusions

To our knowledge, this is the fourth definitive case of RHBLS reported. This report aims to raise awareness about RHBLS, a rare but distinct cutaneous sequela of radiotherapy. Given that radiotherapy is widely used as a primary and adjuvant treatment for cancer, early consideration of RHBLS is advised in patients with a history of radiotherapy who present with sclerotic skin and hemorrhagic bullae, even years after radiotherapy treatment. This case emphasizes the need for increased clinical vigilance and early intervention to prevent further complications. Given the small sample size of only four, including this one, reported cases in the literature, and limited conclusions, it is difficult to generalize the findings, so caution is needed when interpreting results. Continued research and collection of additional cases of RHBLS are necessary to strengthen the evidence and allow the development of clearer guidelines for the diagnosis and management of this condition. Larger case studies or systematic reviews would also help to better understand this condition.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 71-year-old female
- **Chief Complaint:** Pruritic, firm, and erythematous plaques in the inframammary folds that progressed into painful, hemorrhagic bullae surrounded by porcelain-white skin.
- **History of Present Illness:** The plaques developed over several weeks following whole breast radiotherapy for invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast (2011) and ductal carcinoma in situ with microinvasive carcinoma of the left breast (2019). Initial treatment for presumed intertrigo with topical steroids, nystatin, and zinc oxide yielded no improvement. 
- **Past Medical History:** Invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast (2011), ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) of the left breast with microinvasive carcinoma (2019), cholelithiasis, hyperlipidemia, and carpal tunnel syndrome. Received anastrozole (2011–2016) and letrozole (after 2019). 
- **Personal History:** Not mentioned.
- **Family History:** Not mentioned.
- **Physical Examination:** Large, painful, hemorrhagic bullae surrounded by porcelain-white skin over the breasts and inframammary folds, extending to areas outside the radiation field, including axillae and inguinal folds.
- **Ancillary Tests:** Biopsies performed on a hemorrhagic bulla and sclerotic skin revealed pauci-immune subepidermal bulla and histological features of lichen sclerosus without excessive collagen deposition seen in morphea.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **RHBLS (Radiation-Induced Hemorrhagic Bullous Lichen Sclerosus):**
   - Included based on the development of lesions at radiation-treated sites, expansion to adjacent areas, and biopsy findings of subepidermal bullae and histological features consistent with lichen sclerosus.
   - Porcelain-white skin was a characteristic finding.

2. **Bullous Morphea:**
   - Considered due to similar histological features and sclerosis but excluded based on the absence of excessive collagen deposition characteristic of morphea.

3. **Chronic Radiation Dermatitis:**
   - Excluded as histopathology in such cases typically shows atypical fibroblasts and dilated vessels with swollen endothelial cells, which were not present.

4. **Angiosarcoma:**
   - Excluded given the absence of irregular vascular pathology on histology.

Other considerations included friction-related trauma from bra use and non-radiation-related lichen sclerosus; however, the timing and location of lesions supported radiotherapy as the primary trigger.

results.content:
### Reasoning:

Step 1: I need to carefully analyze the patient's case to make an accurate diagnosis. The patient is a 71-year-old female with a history of breast cancer and recent radiotherapy. She's presenting with pruritic, firm, erythematous plaques that have progressed into painful, hemorrhagic bullae surrounded by porcelain-white skin in the inframammary folds.

Step 2: Let's consider the timeline. The plaques developed over several weeks following whole breast radiotherapy for breast cancer. She had invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast in 2011 and ductal carcinoma in situ with microinvasive carcinoma of the left breast in 2019. She was treated with anastrozole from 2011 to 2016 and letrozole after 2019.

Step 3: The initial treatment for presumed intertrigo with topical steroids, nystatin, and zinc oxide didn't improve her condition. This suggests that the underlying cause might not be fungal or bacterial, and the use of steroids might have even aggravated the condition if it's not intertrigo.

Step 4: Physical examination reveals large, painful, hemorrhagic bullae surrounded by porcelain-white skin over the breasts and inframammary folds, extending to areas outside the radiation field, including axillae and inguinal folds. This distribution is important because it's not confined to the radiation field, suggesting a possible systemic or immune-mediated process.

Step 5: Ancillary tests include biopsies that show a pauci-immune subepidermal bulla and histological features of lichen sclerosus without excessive collagen deposition seen in morphea. This is crucial information.

Step 6: Let's consider the differential diagnoses based on the clinical presentation and biopsy findings.

Step 7: First, radiation dermatitis could be considered, but the lesions are progressing and extending beyond the radiation field, which is atypical for routine radiation dermatitis.

Step 8: Bullous pemphigoid is a possibility, given the presence of subepidermal bullae, but typically it presents with lesions in flexural areas and is more common in the elderly. However, the biopsy shows pauci-immune deposits, which is不像典型的bullous pemphigoid.

Step 9: Another consideration is lichen sclerosus, which is known to affect mucous membranes and skin, particularly in postmenopausal women. The biopsy shows features of lichen sclerosus, which aligns with this possibility.

Step 10: However, lichen sclerosus typically doesn't present with hemorrhagic bullae. It usually presents with whitish, atrophic skin with pruritus.

Step 11: Given the history of breast cancer and radiotherapy, could there be a relationship between the cancer treatment and the current skin condition?

Step 12: It's known that aromatase inhibitors like anastrozole and letrozole can have side effects including skin rash and pruritus, but typically not bullous eruptions.

Step 13: The extension of lesions beyond the radiation field suggests a process that isn't purely radiation-induced.

Step 14: The biopsy showing a pauci-immune subepidermal bulla could suggest a subepidermal blistering disease, but the absence of significant immune deposits makes some bullous conditions less likely.

Step 15: Could this be a case of lichen sclerosus et atrophicus with secondary bullous changes due to trauma or another factor?

Step 16: Alternatively, is there a possibility of a mixed process, where lichen sclerosus is present along with another condition causing the bullae?

Step 17: The porcelain-white skin surrounding the bullae could be due to atrophy associated with lichen sclerosus or possibly due to the healing process of the bullae.

Step 18: It's also important to consider other causes of subepidermal bullae, such as bullous systemic lupus erythematosus or dermatitis herpetiformis, but these typically have different clinical and histological features.

Step 19: Given the patient's history of cancer and treatment, could there be a paraneoplastic process affecting the skin?

Step 20: However, the patient's cancers were treated, and it's been several years since the last treatment, making a paraneoplastic cause less likely.

Step 21: Another consideration is that the radiotherapy may have triggered or exacerbated an underlying skin condition like lichen sclerosus.

Step 22: Radiation can cause or reactivate certain skin conditions, and lichen sclerosus has been rarely reported after radiation therapy.

Step 23: The progression from plaques to bullae could indicate a chronic inflammatory process leading to blister formation.

Step 24: The use of topical steroids initially might have temporarily suppressed inflammation but didn't resolve the underlying issue, and possibly contributed to the progression to bullae.

Step 25: Given that the lesions are painful and hemorrhagic, there may be an element of fragility in the skin, which is consistent with lichen sclerosus, known for causing thinning and fragility of the skin.

Step 26: It's also important to consider if there could be an infectious component, but the treatment with nystatin didn't improve the condition, suggesting that fungal infection isn't the primary issue.

Step 27: Given all these considerations, the most likely diagnosis is lichen sclerosus with bullous changes, possibly aggravated by radiotherapy and the use of topical steroids.

Step 28: Therefore, the final diagnosis should be lichen sclerosus with bullous complications.

### Answer:

Lichen sclerosus with bullous complications

<Step 2> Porcelain-white skin was identified as a characteristic finding supporting the diagnosis of RHBLS.
<Step 3> Bullous morphea was considered due to similar histological features and sclerosis but excluded based on the absence of excessive collagen deposition characteristic of morphea.
<Step 4> Chronic radiation dermatitis was excluded as histopathology typically shows atypical fibroblasts and dilated vessels with swollen endothelial cells, which were not present.
<Step 5> Angiosarcoma was excluded given the absence of irregular vascular pathology on histology.

<Step 6> Friction-related trauma from bra use and non-radiation-related lichen sclerosus were considered but excluded due to the timing and location of lesions supporting radiotherapy as the primary trigger.
<Step 7> Final diagnosis: Radiation-Induced Hemorrhagic Bullous Lichen Sclerosus (RHBLS).
